question:
Let $S_0$ be a finite set of positive integers.  We define finite
sets
$S_1,S_2,\ldots$ of positive integers as follows:
the integer $a$ is in $S_{n+1}$ if and only if exactly one of $a-1$ or $a$ is
in
$S_n$.
Show that there exist infinitely many integers $N$ for which
$S_N=S_0\cup\{N+a: a\in S_0\}$.
solution:
We claim that all integers $N$ of the form $2^k$, with $k$ a positive
integer and $N>\max\{S_0\}$, satisfy the desired conditions.

It follows from the definition of $S_n$, and induction on $n$, that
\begin{align*}
\sum_{j \in S_n} x^j &\equiv (1+x) \sum_{j \in S_{n-1}} x^j \\
&\equiv (1+x)^n \sum_{j \in S_0} x^j \pmod{2}.
\end{align*}
From the identity $(x+y)^2 \equiv x^2+y^2 \pmod{2}$ and induction
on $n$, we have $(x+y)^{2^n} \equiv x^{2^n} + y^{2^n} \pmod{2}$.
Hence if we choose $N$ to be a power of 2 greater than $\max\{S_0\}$, then
\[
\sum_{j \in S_n} \equiv (1+x^N) \sum_{j \in S_0} x^j
\]
and $S_N=S_0\cup\{N+a: a\in S_0\}$, as desired.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $initialset$ be a finite set of positive integers.  We define finite
sets
$S_1,S_2,\ldots$ of positive integers as follows:
the integer $elemint$ is in $S_{stepnum+1}$ if and only if exactly one of $elemint-1$ or $elemint$ is
in
$stepset$.
Show that there exist infinitely many integers $largenum$ for which
$S_{largenum}=initialset\cup\{largenum+elemint: elemint\in initialset\}$.
descriptive_long.solution:
We claim that all integers $largenum$ of the form $2^{powindex}$, with $powindex$ a positive
integer and $largenum>\max\{initialset\}$, satisfy the desired conditions.

It follows from the definition of $stepset$, and induction on $stepnum$, that
\begin{align*}
\sum_{loopvar \in stepset} indetvar^{loopvar} &\equiv (1+indetvar) \sum_{loopvar \in prevset} indetvar^{loopvar} \\
&\equiv (1+indetvar)^{stepnum} \sum_{loopvar \in initialset} indetvar^{loopvar} \pmod{2}.
\end{align*}
From the identity $(indetvar+auxvar)^2 \equiv indetvar^2+auxvar^2 \pmod{2}$ and induction
on $stepnum$, we have $(indetvar+auxvar)^{2^{stepnum}} \equiv indetvar^{2^{stepnum}} + auxvar^{2^{stepnum}} \pmod{2}$.
Hence if we choose $largenum$ to be a power of 2 greater than $\max\{initialset\}$, then
\[
\sum_{loopvar \in stepset} indetvar^{loopvar} \equiv (1+indetvar^{largenum}) \sum_{loopvar \in initialset} indetvar^{loopvar}
\]
and $S_{largenum}=initialset\cup\{largenum+elemint: elemint\in initialset\}$, as desired.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $butterfly$ be a finite set of positive integers.  We define finite
sets
$S_1,S_2,\ldots$ of positive integers as follows:
the integer $lighthouse$ is in $S_{sailboat+1}$ if and only if exactly one of $lighthouse-1$ or $lighthouse$ is
in
$raincloud$.
Show that there exist infinitely many integers $blacksmith$ for which
$S_{blacksmith}=butterfly\cup\{blacksmith+lighthouse: lighthouse\in butterfly\}$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We claim that all integers $blacksmith$ of the form $2^{pineapple}$, with $pineapple$ a positive
integer and $blacksmith>\max\{butterfly\}$, satisfy the desired conditions.

It follows from the definition of $raincloud$, and induction on $sailboat$, that
\begin{align*}
\sum_{marigold \in raincloud} macaroon^{marigold} &\equiv (1+macaroon) \sum_{marigold \in S_{sailboat-1}} macaroon^{marigold} \\
&\equiv (1+macaroon)^{sailboat} \sum_{marigold \in butterfly} macaroon^{marigold} \pmod{2}.
\end{align*}
From the identity $(macaroon+goldfinch)^2 \equiv macaroon^2+goldfinch^2 \pmod{2}$ and induction
on $sailboat$, we have $(macaroon+goldfinch)^{2^{sailboat}} \equiv macaroon^{2^{sailboat}} + goldfinch^{2^{sailboat}} \pmod{2}$.
Hence if we choose $blacksmith$ to be a power of 2 greater than $\max\{butterfly\}$, then
\[
\sum_{marigold \in raincloud} \equiv (1+macaroon^{blacksmith}) \sum_{marigold \in butterfly} macaroon^{marigold}
\]
and $S_{blacksmith}=butterfly\cup\{blacksmith+lighthouse: lighthouse\in butterfly\}$, as desired.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $terminalset$ be a finite set of positive integers.  We define finite
sets
$S_1,S_2,\ldots$ of positive integers as follows:
the integer $\omegaindex$ is in $S_{constantless+1}$ if and only if exactly one of $\omegaindex-1$ or $\omegaindex$ is
in
$emptygroup$.
Show that there exist infinitely many integers $\minvalue$ for which
$S_{\minvalue}=terminalset\cup\{\minvalue+\omegaindex: \omegaindex\in terminalset\}$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We claim that all integers $\minvalue$ of the form $2^{groundfloor}$, with $groundfloor$ a positive
integer and $\minvalue>\max\{terminalset\}$, satisfy the desired conditions.

It follows from the definition of $emptygroup$, and induction on $constantless$, that
\begin{align*}
\sum_{outsider \in emptygroup} knownvalue^{outsider} &\equiv (1+knownvalue) \sum_{outsider \in fullsetprev} knownvalue^{outsider} \\&\equiv (1+knownvalue)^{constantless} \sum_{outsider \in terminalset} knownvalue^{outsider} \pmod{2}.
\end{align*}
From the identity $(knownvalue+horizontal)^2 \equiv knownvalue^2+horizontal^2 \pmod{2}$ and induction
on $constantless$, we have $(knownvalue+horizontal)^{2^{constantless}} \equiv knownvalue^{2^{constantless}} + horizontal^{2^{constantless}} \pmod{2}$.
Hence if we choose $\minvalue$ to be a power of 2 greater than $\max\{terminalset\}$, then
\[\sum_{outsider \in emptygroup} \equiv (1+knownvalue^{\minvalue}) \sum_{outsider \in terminalset} knownvalue^{outsider}\]
and $S_{\minvalue}=terminalset\cup\{\minvalue+\omegaindex: \omegaindex\in terminalset\}$, as desired.

garbled_string.question:
<<<
Let $wsfvbkjo$ be a finite set of positive integers.  We define finite
sets
$S_1,S_2,\ldots$ of positive integers as follows:
the integer $qzxwvtnp$ is in $S_{vchztbru+1}$ if and only if exactly one of $qzxwvtnp-1$ or $qzxwvtnp$ is
in
$uzldamfs$.
Show that there exist infinitely many integers $dzkqrhpe$ for which
$S_{dzkqrhpe}=wsfvbkjo\cup\{dzkqrhpe+qzxwvtnp: qzxwvtnp\in wsfvbkjo\}$.
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
<<<
We claim that all integers $dzkqrhpe$ of the form $2^{mndfplqe}$, with $mndfplqe$ a positive
integer and $dzkqrhpe>\max\{wsfvbkjo\}$, satisfy the desired conditions.

It follows from the definition of $uzldamfs$, and induction on $vchztbru$, that
\begin{align*}
\sum_{hjgrksla \in uzldamfs} sbclwrmd^{hjgrksla} &\equiv (1+sbclwrmd) \sum_{hjgrksla \in rcnwtopg} sbclwrmd^{hjgrksla} \\
&\equiv (1+sbclwrmd)^{vchztbru} \sum_{hjgrksla \in wsfvbkjo} sbclwrmd^{hjgrksla} \pmod{2}.
\end{align*}
From the identity $(sbclwrmd+ptkrvngx)^2 \equiv sbclwrmd^2+ptkrvngx^2 \pmod{2}$ and induction
on $vchztbru$, we have $(sbclwrmd+ptkrvngx)^{2^{vchztbru}} \equiv sbclwrmd^{2^{vchztbru}} + ptkrvngx^{2^{vchztbru}} \pmod{2}$.
Hence if we choose $dzkqrhpe$ to be a power of 2 greater than $\max\{wsfvbkjo\}$, then
\[
\sum_{hjgrksla \in uzldamfs} sbclwrmd^{hjgrksla} \equiv (1+sbclwrmd^{dzkqrhpe}) \sum_{hjgrksla \in wsfvbkjo} sbclwrmd^{hjgrksla}
\]
and $S_{dzkqrhpe}=wsfvbkjo\cup\{dzkqrhpe+qzxwvtnp: qzxwvtnp\in wsfvbkjo\}$, as desired.
>>>

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer m \geq  2 and a positive integer d.  
For every point v = (v_1,\ldots ,v_m) with positive integer coordinates denote by e_i the i-th unit vector, so v - d e_i is obtained from v by subtracting d from the i-th coordinate.

Let S_0 be a finite subset of \mathbb{Z}_{>0}^m.  
For n \geq  0 define S_{n+1} \subset  \mathbb{Z}_{>0}^m by the rule

 v \in  S_{n+1} \Leftrightarrow    | { v } \cup  { v - d e_i : 1\leq i\leq m } \cap  S_n |  is odd. (\star )

(If a point occurring in (\star ) has a non-positive coordinate, it is interpreted as not belonging to S_n.)

Prove that there are infinitely many positive integers N such that

 S_N  =  S_0  \cup   \bigcup _{i=1}^m { v + dN e_i : v \in  S_0 }.  ()

Equivalently, show that for every k with d\cdot 2^k  >  max{ coordinate of any point of S_0 } one has

 S_{2^k}  =  S_0  \cup   \bigcup _{i=1}^m { v + d\cdot 2^k e_i : v \in  S_0 }.  (')
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 - Encoding sets by multivariate polynomials over F_2.  
For a finite set T \subset  \mathbb{Z}_{>0}^m define its generating polynomial

 P_T(X_1,\ldots ,X_m)  =  \sum _{(a_1,\ldots ,a_m)\in T}  X_1^{a_1}\cdot \cdot \cdot X_m^{a_m}  \in  F_2[X_1,\ldots ,X_m].

Write P_n for P_{S_n}.  We work throughout in the polynomial ring R := F_2[X_1,\ldots ,X_m], where all identities are taken modulo 2.

Step 2 - Translating the evolution rule.  
Fix L(X_1,\ldots ,X_m) := 1 + X_1^{d} + \cdots  + X_m^{d} \in  R.  
We claim:

 P_{n+1}  =  L \cdot  P_n  for every n \geq  0. (1)

Indeed, let v = (a_1,\ldots ,a_m) with all a_j > 0.  
In P_n the monomials whose product with L contribute to the coefficient of X_1^{a_1}\ldots X_m^{a_m} are

* X_1^{a_1}\ldots X_m^{a_m}  (corresponding to v itself),  
* X_1^{a_1-d} X_2^{a_2}\ldots X_m^{a_m}  (corresponding to v - d e_1),  
  \ldots    
* X_1^{a_1}\ldots X_{m-1}^{a_{m-1}} X_m^{a_m-d}  (corresponding to v - d e_m).

Hence the coefficient of that monomial in L\cdot P_n is precisely the parity described in (\star ); (1) follows.

Step 3 - Iterating the operator.  
By (1),

 P_n  =  L^n \cdot  P_0. (2)

Because we work in characteristic 2, the Frobenius endomorphism gives

 ( A + B )^{2}  =  A^{2} + B^{2},  and by induction  
 ( A + B_1 + \cdots  + B_t )^{2^k}  =  A^{2^k} + B_1^{2^k} + \cdots  + B_t^{2^k}. (3)

Applying (3) with A = 1 and B_i = X_i^{d} we obtain

 L^{2^k}  =  (1 + X_1^{d} + \cdots  + X_m^{d})^{2^k}
     =  1 + X_1^{d\cdot 2^k} + \cdots  + X_m^{d\cdot 2^k}. (4)

Step 4 - Description of S_{2^k}.  
Insert (4) into (2):

 P_{2^k}  =  (1 + X_1^{d\cdot 2^k} + \cdots  + X_m^{d\cdot 2^k}) \cdot  P_0
     =  P_0 + \sum _{i=1}^{m} X_i^{d\cdot 2^k} P_0. (5)

Interpretation of each term:

* P_0 corresponds to S_0;  
* X_i^{d\cdot 2^k} P_0 is obtained by multiplying every monomial of P_0 by X_i^{d\cdot 2^k}, i.e. by adding d\cdot 2^k to the i-th coordinate of each point of S_0.  Consequently

 X_i^{d\cdot 2^k} P_0 \leftrightarrow  { v + d\cdot 2^k e_i : v \in  S_0 }.

Thus (5) states exactly that S_{2^k} is the union described in (').

Step 5 - Ensuring disjointness and positivity.  
Because d\cdot 2^k exceeds every coordinate appearing in S_0, all shifted copies in (') lie outside S_0 and outside one another, and all coordinates remain positive.  Hence (') holds as an equality of sets.

Step 6 - Infinitely many suitable N.  
The inequality d\cdot 2^k > max coordinate of S_0 holds for every k greater than some fixed k_0, giving infinitely many powers N = 2^k that satisfy ().  \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension / more variables:  
   The problem now takes place in ℤ_{>0}^m with an arbitrary dimension m ≥ 2 instead of the original one–dimensional setting.  The state of each point depends simultaneously on m+1 neighbouring points, vastly increasing the combinatorial complexity.

2. Additional shifts and interactions:  
   The update rule couples the current point with m different d-shifts, creating m+1 interacting “directions’’ rather than a single one.  The final pattern (♣) contains m+1 distinct, pairwise disjoint copies of the initial configuration, not merely one.

3. More sophisticated mathematics:  
   Solving the problem demands working in the multivariate polynomial ring 𝔽₂[X₁,…,X_m] and exploiting the Frobenius endomorphism in characteristic 2.  Handling several commuting variables simultaneously and interpreting their exponents as m-dimensional translations is substantially subtler than the univariate case.

4. Deeper theoretical insight:  
   One must recognise that the operator L = 1 + Σ X_i^{d} encodes the cellular-automaton rule, observe that iterates of L at powers of 2 “linearise’’ via the Frobenius map, and translate polynomial multiplications back into geometric statements about lattice sets.  This blend of algebra, combinatorics, and geometry goes beyond the elementary manipulations sufficient for the original problem.

5. Increased proof length and number of steps:  
   The solution requires six logically distinct steps (encoding, translation, iteration, Frobenius analysis, geometric interpretation, infinitude argument), each introducing non-trivial ideas.  In the original one-variable situation, two or three steps suffice.

For these reasons the enhanced kernel variant is significantly harder than both the original problem and the given kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer m \geq  2 and a positive integer d.  
For every point v = (v_1,\ldots ,v_m) with positive integer coordinates denote by e_i the i-th unit vector, so v - d e_i is obtained from v by subtracting d from the i-th coordinate.

Let S_0 be a finite subset of \mathbb{Z}_{>0}^m.  
For n \geq  0 define S_{n+1} \subset  \mathbb{Z}_{>0}^m by the rule

 v \in  S_{n+1} \Leftrightarrow    | { v } \cup  { v - d e_i : 1\leq i\leq m } \cap  S_n |  is odd. (\star )

(If a point occurring in (\star ) has a non-positive coordinate, it is interpreted as not belonging to S_n.)

Prove that there are infinitely many positive integers N such that

 S_N  =  S_0  \cup   \bigcup _{i=1}^m { v + dN e_i : v \in  S_0 }.  ()

Equivalently, show that for every k with d\cdot 2^k  >  max{ coordinate of any point of S_0 } one has

 S_{2^k}  =  S_0  \cup   \bigcup _{i=1}^m { v + d\cdot 2^k e_i : v \in  S_0 }.  (')

 P_T(X_1,\ldots ,X_m)  =  \sum _{(a_1,\ldots ,a_m)\in T}  X_1^{a_1}\cdot \cdot \cdot X_m^{a_m}  \in  F_2[X_1,\ldots ,X_m].

Write P_n for P_{S_n}.  We work throughout in the polynomial ring R := F_2[X_1,\ldots ,X_m], where all identities are taken modulo 2.

Step 2 - Translating the evolution rule.

* X_1^{a_1}\ldots X_m^{a_m}  (corresponding to v itself),  
* X_1^{a_1-d} X_2^{a_2}\ldots X_m^{a_m}  (corresponding to v - d e_1),  
  \ldots    
* X_1^{a_1}\ldots X_{m-1}^{a_{m-1}} X_m^{a_m-d}  (corresponding to v - d e_m).

Hence the coefficient of that monomial in L\cdot P_n is precisely the parity described in (\star ); (1) follows.

Step 3 - Iterating the operator.  
By (1),

 P_n  =  L^n \cdot  P_0. (2)

Because we work in characteristic 2, the Frobenius endomorphism gives

 ( A + B )^{2}  =  A^{2} + B^{2},  and by induction

Applying (3) with A = 1 and B_i = X_i^{d} we obtain

 L^{2^k}  =  (1 + X_1^{d} + \cdots  + X_m^{d})^{2^k}
     =  1 + X_1^{d\cdot 2^k} + \cdots  + X_m^{d\cdot 2^k}. (4)

Step 4 - Description of S_{2^k}.  
Insert (4) into (2):

 P_{2^k}  =  (1 + X_1^{d\cdot 2^k} + \cdots  + X_m^{d\cdot 2^k}) \cdot  P_0
     =  P_0 + \sum _{i=1}^{m} X_i^{d\cdot 2^k} P_0. (5)

* P_0 corresponds to S_0;

Because d\cdot 2^k exceeds every coordinate appearing in S_0, all shifted copies in (') lie outside S_0 and outside one another, and all coordinates remain positive.  Hence (') holds as an equality of sets.

Step 6 - Infinitely many suitable N.  
The inequality d\cdot 2^k > max coordinate of S_0 holds for every k greater than some fixed k_0, giving infinitely many powers N = 2^k that satisfy ().  \blacksquare
